summary: A brief reflection on the life and legacy of Hildegard of Bingen, highlighting her multifaceted expertise across theology, science, and the arts. The text emphasizes her complex character, noting her integration of reason and mystery, her prophetic dissent, and her reliance on divine inspiration to articulate her visions.

principles.statement: The universe is ordered, deeply rooted in its creator, and humanity holds a deeply integrated place within it.
principles.rationale: She viewed the cosmos and humanity as interconnected and grounded in God, blending the scientific understanding of her day with orthodox theology.
She refused to exhume the body of an excommunicated man because she believed he had died reconciled to God, accepting her own temporary excommunication as a result.

signature_quotes.text: Already as a child, I saw great things of wonder which my tongue could never have given expression to, if God’s spirit hadn’t taught me to believe.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on her early visions and her reliance on divine guidance to articulate them.
